paper_title: Material and methods from ICDP expedition PISDP 2006

methods.description: Process for collecting and storing sediment cores from Lake Petén-Itzá during the ICDP expedition PISDP in 2006.

steps.description: Sediment cores were collected at site PI-6 in Lake Petén-Itzá.
steps.searchable_summary: collect sediment cores from Lake Petén-Itzá

steps.description: Cores were stored on board at 5°C, then transferred to the Minneapolis Core-Repository in Minnesota (USA).
steps.searchable_summary: store and transfer sediment cores to repository

methods.method_name: Sediment Core Dating and Age Model Derivation
methods.description: Dating of sediment cores using 14C and Ar/Ar methods and deriving an age model.

steps.description: The core was dated by 14C (44 ages) on terrestrial remains and three tephra layers dated by Ar/Ar.
steps.searchable_summary: date sediment cores using 14C and Ar/Ar methods

steps.description: An age model was derived using a weighted fit through 36 age-depth points.
steps.searchable_summary: derive age model using weighted fit

methods.description: Preparation of diatom samples from sediment cores for light and scanning electron microscopy.

steps.description: A 5 mm thick slice of sediment was decarbonated with hot 37% HCl for a few hours.
The organic matter was oxidized with hot 33% H2O2 for a few hours.
Diatom suspensions were successively rinsed and decanted with distilled water.
Aliquots of cleaned diatoms were diluted and evaporated at room temperature onto coverslips.
steps.searchable_summary: dilute and evaporate diatoms onto coverslips

steps.description: Diatom slides were examined under oil immersion using light microscopy at 630× or 1000× magnification.
steps.searchable_summary: examine diatom slides with light microscopy

steps.description: For SEM observations, cleaned diatom material was air-dried on coverslips, attached to stubs, and gold-coated.
steps.searchable_summary: prepare diatoms for SEM by air-drying and gold-coating

steps.description: Diatoms were examined with a XL 30 ESEM Philips SEM at an accelerating voltage of 1-30 kV.
